_about: SAMPLE cached transfer-questions so check/eval/baselines/leakage/paraphrase run today with NO API key. Replace by running `python -m speedrun.ai.run generate` with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set. Four items are DELIBERATELY defective to prove the checker blocks bad cards: gen-bio-cellresp-1 (wrong fact), gen-bio-cellresp-2 (near-copy of source), gen-bio-membrane-3 (duplicate choices), gen-bio-enzyme-3 (ungrounded).

items.stem: A scientist follows where the six-carbon fuel is first split into two three-carbon fragments within a cell. Where does that first splitting take place?
items.rationale: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and splits one glucose into two pyruvate molecules before later stages enter the mitochondrion.

items.stem: During aerobic metabolism, which species ultimately accepts the spent electrons at the very end of the transport chain?
choices: Oxygen, which is reduced to water
items.rationale: Carbon dioxide serves as the terminal acceptor at the end of the chain.

items.stem: ATP synthase uses the resulting proton gradient to phosphorylate ADP into ATP through oxidative phosphorylation, which yields the large majority of the roughly 30 to 32 ATP produced per glucose; roughly how many ATP are produced per glucose?
items.rationale: Oxidative phosphorylation yields the large majority of the roughly 30 to 32 ATP produced per glucose.

items.stem: A protein sitting in the inner membrane taps a flow of protons to bolt a phosphate onto ADP. Which protein does this?
items.rationale: ATP synthase uses the proton gradient to phosphorylate ADP into ATP through oxidative phosphorylation.

items.stem: Which conceptual picture best captures how lipids and proteins are arranged in a cell's outer boundary?
items.rationale: The plasma membrane is described by the fluid mosaic model: a fluid phospholipid bilayer studded with embedded proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates.

items.stem: For every molecule of energy currency it consumes, how many sodium ions does the sodium-potassium pump push out of the cell?
choices: Three sodium ions exported (and two potassium ions imported)
items.rationale: The sodium-potassium pump exports three sodium ions and imports two potassium ions per ATP hydrolyzed, maintaining the electrochemical gradient.

items.stem: Pushing a solute uphill from a dilute region to a concentrated one across a membrane demands which category of transport?
items.rationale: Active transport moves substances against their gradient and requires ATP, unlike passive transport down a gradient.

items.stem: Which part of a phospholipid molecule turns toward the watery surroundings on either side of the membrane?
items.rationale: Each phospholipid has a hydrophilic phosphate head facing the aqueous environment and two hydrophobic fatty-acid tails facing inward.

items.stem: After a molecule is copied, every product helix retains one old chain paired with one brand-new chain. What name describes this inheritance pattern?
choices: Semiconservative: each daughter has one parental and one new strand
items.rationale: DNA replication is semiconservative: each daughter molecule contains one original parental strand and one newly synthesized strand.

items.stem: Along the template, in which orientation does the copying enzyme keep adding nucleotides to a growing chain?
items.rationale: DNA polymerase synthesizes new strands only in the five-prime to three-prime direction, giving a continuous leading strand.

items.stem: When a stretch of DNA is transcribed into a messenger molecule, which building block stands in for thymine?
items.rationale: Transcription copies a gene's DNA template into messenger RNA, with uracil replacing thymine.

items.stem: Protein synthesis kicks off at one particular three-letter signal. Which triplet is it, and which residue does it recruit first?
items.rationale: The start codon AUG codes for methionine, read from the messenger RNA at the ribosome.

items.stem: Through what route does a biological catalyst make a reaction go faster while emerging unchanged afterward?
choices: By raising the reaction's equilibrium constant
By adding heat to the system
By being consumed as a reactant
items.rationale: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed reactions by lowering the activation energy without being consumed or changing equilibrium.

items.stem: Within Michaelis-Menten behavior, which value corresponds to the substrate level giving half the top reaction speed?
choices: The substrate concentration at half of Vmax
items.rationale: Km is the substrate concentration at half of Vmax, so a lower Km indicates higher affinity.

items.stem: A rival molecule vies with the substrate for the same pocket on an enzyme. What is the effect on the top speed and the apparent Km?
choices: Vmax is unchanged while apparent Km increases
Vmax falls while Km is unchanged
Both Vmax and Km are unchanged
items.rationale: A competitive inhibitor binds the active site and can be overcome by more substrate, raising the apparent Km while leaving Vmax unchanged.

items.stem: If enzyme A shows a far smaller Km than enzyme B toward the same substrate, what does that reveal about enzyme A?
choices: It works only at high pH

items.stem: While idle, the interior of a typical nerve cell rests at roughly what electrical potential compared with the outside?
items.rationale: A neuron maintains a resting membrane potential of about negative seventy millivolts, kept negative by the sodium-potassium pump and potassium leak channels.

items.stem: Which charged particle pours inward to power the upstroke of a nerve impulse once threshold is crossed?
choices: Sodium ions through voltage-gated sodium channels
items.rationale: When threshold is reached voltage-gated sodium channels open and sodium rushes in, producing the rising phase of the action potential.

items.stem: In an insulated axon the signal appears to hop from one bare gap to the next. What is this speed-boosting style of propagation called?
items.rationale: In myelinated axons the impulse jumps between nodes of Ranvier in saltatory conduction, greatly increasing speed.

items.stem: Two residues bond together and expel a molecule of water in the process. What is the linkage that results?
choices: A peptide bond, which is an amide bond
items.rationale: Amino acids link through peptide bonds, which are amide bonds formed by a condensation reaction that releases water.

items.stem: What kind of interaction chiefly props up the coils and pleated regions of a protein's local shape?
choices: Hydrogen bonds along the polypeptide backbone
Covalent carbon-carbon bonds in the side chains
Van der Waals forces between water molecules
items.rationale: Secondary structure includes alpha helices and beta sheets stabilized by hydrogen bonds along the backbone.

items.stem: From how many distinct standard residues does a cell assemble its proteins?
items.rationale: Proteins are polymers of twenty standard amino acids, each with a distinctive side chain.

items.stem: Two simple sugars unite while a water molecule is removed. What sort of bond ties them together?
choices: A glycosidic bond formed by dehydration synthesis
items.rationale: Monosaccharides join through glycosidic bonds formed by dehydration synthesis.

items.stem: In what branched storage form do animals bank their surplus glucose?
items.rationale: Storage polysaccharides include starch in plants and glycogen in animals, both polymers of glucose.

items.stem: The rigid fiber of plant walls is glucose strung together by which specific linkage that people cannot break down?
items.rationale: Cellulose is a polymer of glucose joined by beta glycosidic linkages that most animals cannot digest.

items.stem: How is the acidity of a solution written mathematically in relation to its hydrogen ion concentration?
choices: The negative base-ten logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration
The square root of the hydrogen ion concentration
The natural logarithm of hydroxide concentration
The product of temperature and concentration
items.rationale: pH equals the negative base-ten logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration.

items.stem: Comparing two acids, how would you describe the one whose pKa is lower?
items.rationale: A larger Ka, or equivalently a smaller pKa, indicates a stronger acid.

items.stem: A solution that fights off shifts in acidity is built from which combination of species?
choices: A weak acid and its conjugate base
A strong acid and a strong base
items.rationale: A buffer resists changes in pH and consists of a weak acid and its conjugate base.

items.stem: A buffered solution shows its strongest ability to absorb added acid or base under which condition tied to pKa?
choices: Only at a pH of exactly seven
items.rationale: A buffer works best when pH is near its pKa and the acid and conjugate base concentrations are approximately equal.

items.stem: For a change to happen on its own without outside help, what must be true of its Gibbs free energy change?
items.rationale: A reaction is spontaneous when the Gibbs free energy change is negative.

items.stem: A process that releases warmth to its surroundings carries an enthalpy change of which sign?
items.rationale: An exothermic reaction releases heat and has a negative enthalpy change.

items.stem: In thermodynamic terms, what property is being described when we talk about the spreading out of energy or randomness?
choices: Disorder, or the dispersal of energy
items.rationale: Entropy is a measure of disorder or the dispersal of energy, and it tends to increase in spontaneous processes.

items.stem: For a gas behaving ideally, what single relationship ties its four state variables together?
choices: Pressure times volume equals moles times the gas constant times absolute temperature
Energy equals mass times the speed of light squared
items.rationale: The ideal gas law states that pressure times volume equals the number of moles times the gas constant times absolute temperature.

items.stem: Keeping heat steady, if you compress a trapped gas into a smaller space, how do its squeeze and its size relate?
choices: They are inversely proportional, as stated by Boyle's law
items.rationale: At constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional, which is Boyle's law.

items.stem: Given a blend of gases that do not react, how do you obtain the combined pressure from the separate ones?
choices: Total pressure equals the sum of the partial pressures of the components
Total pressure equals the product of the partial pressures
Total pressure equals the average of the partial pressures
Total pressure equals the largest partial pressure only
items.rationale: Dalton's law of partial pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of its components.

items.stem: What arrangement around a single carbon atom qualifies it as a stereocenter?
choices: A carbon bonded to four different groups
A carbon bonded to two identical groups
A carbon in a double bond
A carbon with a lone pair
items.rationale: A chiral center, or stereocenter, is a carbon bonded to four different groups.

items.stem: When a nucleophile strikes an unhindered carbon in one smooth concerted step, what becomes of the three-dimensional arrangement at that carbon?
items.rationale: An SN2 reaction is a concerted mechanism that proceeds with inversion of configuration.

items.stem: A crowded tertiary carbon reacts over two steps by way of a positively charged carbon species. What is that fleeting species?
items.rationale: An SN1 reaction proceeds through a carbocation intermediate and is favored by tertiary substrates.

items.stem: The defining feature of a carbonyl is a carbon tied by a double bond to which element?
items.rationale: A carbonyl group is a carbon double-bonded to oxygen, appearing in aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids.

items.stem: How does the overall push on a body connect to how heavy it is and how fast its motion changes?
choices: Net force equals mass times acceleration
Net force equals mass divided by velocity
Net force equals weight times height
Net force equals momentum times time
items.rationale: Newton's second law states that the net force on an object equals its mass times its acceleration.

items.stem: The total work performed on a moving body is equal to the change in which quantity?
choices: The net work done equals the change in kinetic energy
items.rationale: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

items.stem: How do you express the energy that an object carries purely because it is moving?
choices: One half mass times velocity squared
items.rationale: Kinetic energy equals one half mass times velocity squared.

items.stem: Across a single resistor, how does the potential difference depend on the flow of charge and the opposition to it?
choices: Voltage equals current divided by resistance
items.rationale: Ohm's law states that the voltage across a resistor equals the current through it times its resistance.

items.stem: If several resistors are wired one after another in a single loop, how is the combined opposition determined?
choices: They add directly as the sum of the individual resistances
You take only the largest value
items.rationale: In a series circuit the total resistance is the sum of the individual resistances.

items.stem: As extra resistors are hung on additional side-by-side branches, what happens to the circuit's overall opposition to current?
choices: Total resistance decreases below the smallest branch resistance
Total resistance stays exactly the same
Total resistance equals the sum of all branches
items.rationale: Adding resistors in parallel decreases total resistance below the smallest branch resistance and increases total current.

items.stem: Taking information in, holding onto it, and later bringing it back name which trio of mental operations?
items.rationale: Memory involves three processes: encoding, the input of information; storage, its retention; and retrieval, getting it back out.

items.stem: Recent bursts of activity durably beef up certain synapses in a way thought to underlie learning. What is this effect named?
items.rationale: Long-term potentiation, a persistent strengthening of synapses based on recent activity, is considered a cellular basis of learning and long-term memory.

items.stem: Why does remembering tend to work better when the setting at test resembles the setting during study?
choices: When cues present at encoding are also present at retrieval, recall improves
Because forgetting is impossible once encoded
Because short-term memory has unlimited space
items.rationale: Retrieval is more successful when cues present at encoding are also present at recall, a principle called encoding specificity.

items.stem: A tone is sounded again and again just before a dog is fed until the tone by itself triggers drooling. What role has the tone taken on?
choices: A conditioned stimulus that elicits a conditioned response
items.rationale: After repeated pairing with food, a neutral stimulus such as a bell becomes a conditioned stimulus that elicits a conditioned response.

items.stem: Rewarding an action only after an unpredictable count of responses produces behavior that is hardest to stamp out. Which reinforcement pattern is that?
items.rationale: Behaviors reinforced on a variable-ratio schedule, where reinforcement follows an unpredictable number of responses, are the most resistant to extinction.
